| **Progress** 05/01/08 to 09/30/12  **Outputs** OUTPUTS: Portions of the results from this research has been posted on Florida's Environmental Public Health Tracking Network. As an example, animated maps of predicted PM2.5 across Florida allow citizens to view the changes in PM2.5 over time. Further, some of the results are disseminated by FDOH personnel to county health professionals. Numerous presentations have been given by the PI as well as students and collaborators to international, national, state and local participants. Below are some key presentations: Invited Presentations: Gotway, Carol A. and Linda J. Young. 2008. Geostatistics: What's Hot, What's Not and Other Food for Thought. 8th International Symposium on Spatial Accuracy Assessment in Natural Resources and Environmental Sciences. Shanghai, China. Young, Linda J. and Carol A. Gotway. 2008. Using geostatistical methods in the analysis of public health data: The final frontier geoENV 2008. Southamption, United Kingdom Gotway, Carol A. and Linda J. Young. 2008. Environmental Public Health Tracking: A Case Study from Florida. Joint Conference of the International Society for Environmental Epidemiology & the International Society of Exposure Analysis. Pasadena, California. Young, Linda J., Carol A. Gotway, Kenneth K. Lopiano, Greg Kearney, Chris DuClos. 2009. Models for assessing uncertainty for local regression: A case study from Florida. 6th St. Petersburg Workshop on Simulation. St. Petersburg, Russia. Gotway, Carol A., Linda J. Young, Kenneth K. Lopiano, Greg Kearney, Chris DuClos. 2009. Models for assessing uncertainty in local regression. GeoMED. Charleston, South Carolina. Young, Linda J., Carol A. Gotway, Kenneth K. Lopiano, Greg Kearney, Chris DuClos. 2009. Relating public health to environmental factors: Assessing uncertainty in the presence of predicted environmental factors. Keynote address at Fields Workshop on Modeling Indirectly and Imprecisely Observed Data. London, Ontario. Young, Linda J., Carol A. Gotway, Kenneth K. Lopiano. 2010. Challenges associated with integrating data from multiple scales to assess relationships. Accuracy 2010. Leicester, United Kingdom. Young, Linda J., Denise Abreu, Pam Arroway, Andrea C. Lamas, and Kenneth K. Lopiano. 2010. Precise estimates of the number of farms in the United States. Joint Statistical Meetings. Vancouver. Young, Linda J., Carol A. Gotway, and Kenneth K. Lopiano. 2010. Challenges associated with integrating data from multiple scales to assess relationships. Compstat. Paris, France. Young, Linda J. 2011. Accounting for Prediction Error in Environmental Exposure when Relating Public Health to Environmental Factors. Eastern Mediterranean Region of the International Biometric Society. Crete. Young, Linda J. 2011. Relating health outcomes to environmental factors: Accounting for prediction error in environmental exposure. 33rd International Conference on Information Technology Interfaces. June 27-30. Cavtat/Dubrovnik, Croatia. Young, Linda J. 2012. ENAR Spring Meeting. Relating public health to environmental factors: Quantifying uncertainty when exposure is predicted. Washington, D.C.. PARTICIPANTS: Carol A. Gotway, U.S. Centers of Disease Control and Prevention Chris DuClos, Florida Department of Health Greg Kearney, Florida Department of Health Melissa Jordan, Florida Department of Health Kenneth Lopiano, University of Florida, graduate student Emily Leary, University of Florida, graduate student A professional development opportunity was provided to statisticians at a 2011 in Crete and to ecologists at a 2011 meeting in Rhode Island. TARGET AUDIENCES: Not relevant to this project. PROJECT MODIFICATIONS: Nothing significant to report during this reporting period.  **Impacts** During the course of this project, methods were developed for combining data collected on disparate spatial scales in such a manner that valid statistical inference can then be conducted. Further, methods of analyses that properly account for the correlation structure induced by predicting exposure so that health outcomes can be related to exposure were developed. These methods have been made accessible to practitioners who conduct studies relating health outcomes to environmental exposures.  **Publications**   * Young, Linda J., Carol A. Gotway, and Kenneth K. Lopiano. 2011. A closer look at errors in variables methods for use in regression models with spatially misaligned data. Communications in Statistics: Simulation and Computation 41: 1250-1269. * Lopiano, Kenneth K., Linda J. Young, and Carol A. Gotway. 2012. Estimated Generalized Least Squares in Spatially-misaligned Regression Models with Berkson Error. Biostatistics. Accepted subject to minor revision. |
| **Progress** 10/01/10 to 09/30/11  **Outputs** OUTPUTS: Methods developed through this research are beginning to be used to provide information for Florida's Environmental Public Health Tracking website. Presentations were made to disseminate the work. Lopiano, Kenneth K., Linda J. Young, and Carol C. Gotway. 2010. Unbiased estimates of uncertainty in general linear regression models with spatially misaligned data. International Biometric Conference. December 5-10. Florianoplis, Brazil. (Selected for oral presentation). Young, Linda J. 2011. Accounting for Prediction Error in Environmental Exposure when Relating Public Health to Environmental Factors. Eastern Mediterranean Region of the International Biometric Society. May 8-12. Crete. (Invited) Young, Linda J. 2011. Relating health outcomes to environmental factors: Accounting for prediction error in environmental exposure. 33rd International Conference on Information Technology Interfaces. June 27-30. Cavtat/Dubrovnik, Croatia. (Invited) Young, Linda J. 2011. Accounting for Prediction Error in Environmental Exposure when Relating Public Health to Environmental Factors. Eastern Mediterranean Region of the International Biometric Society. May 8-12. Crete. (Invited) PARTICIPANTS: Linda J. Young was lead on this project. Graduate students Kenneth K. Lopiano and Emily Leary had relevant research efforts. Personnel at the Florida Department of Health, U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, and the U.S. National Aeronautical and Space Administration collaborated on this work. A full day workshop on these methods was given to biostatisticians in Croatia during June, 2011. TARGET AUDIENCES: The workshop, Relating health outcomes to environmental factors: Accounting for prediction error in environmental exposure, was presented to biostatisticians in Dubrovnik, Croatia. These statisticians were primarily from Croatia and the surrounding countries. PROJECT MODIFICATIONS: Nothing significant to report during this reporting period.  **Impacts** When assessing the association between health outcomes and environmental factors, the data are often misaligned. For example, health outcomes are often reported at the zip code or county level and environmental exposure, such as air quality, is often recorded at points. Before the association between the two can be assessed, they must be joined on a common spatial scale. This generally leads to exposure being predicted for the spatial scale for which health outcomes are observed. Then the association is often evaluated through regression of health outcomes on the predicted exposure. If a smoothing method, such as kriging, is used to make the predictions, the estimate of association is unbiased, but the standard error tends to be much too small. As a consequence, associations are declared significant too often. This could result in incorrectly raising public concerns about environmental effects on health. This year methods were developed so that the association between health outcomes and environmental exposure could be accurately assessed.  **Publications**   * Lopiano, Kenneth K., Carol A. Gotway, and Linda J. Young. 2011. A comparison of errors in variables methods for use in regression models with spatially misaligned data. Statistical Methods in Medical Research 20: 29-47. Doi:10.1177/0962280210370266. |
| **Progress** 10/01/09 to 09/30/10  **Outputs** OUTPUTS: An on-going collaborative relationship exists between this project and Florida's Environmental Public Health Tracking Program (FEPHT), which is housed within Florida's Department of Health. Data management, analyses, and visual displays are provided to FEPHT in this collaborative effort. FEPHT is part of the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's (CDC's) Environmental Public Health Tracking (EPHT) Program, and ties are now being built to the parent organization. As an example, two attended a CDC workshop on software that is being incorporated into EPHT. The following presentations have been given for this project during the reporting period: Young LJ, Gotway CA and Lopiano KK. 2010. Challenges associated with integrating data from multiple scales to assess relationships. Compstat. August 22-27. Paris, France, (Invited) Young LJ, Gotway CA, Lopiano KK. 2010. Challenges associated with integrating data from multiple scales to assess relationships. Accuracy 2010. July 22-24. Leicester, United Kingdom. (Invited) Young LJ, Gotway CA, Lopiano KK, Kearney G and DuClos C. 2009. Relating public health to environmental factors: Assessing uncertainty in the presence of predicted environmental factors. Keynote address at Fields Workshop on Modeling Indirectly and Imprecisely Observed Data. December 10-12. London, Ontario. (Invited) Young LJ, Gotway CA, Xu X, Kearney G and Hyman M. 2009. Assessing the association between public health and environmental factors. Annual Meeting of the Society for Risk Analysis. December 6-9. Baltimore, Maryland. (Invited) Gotway, Carol A., Linda J. Young, Kenneth K. Lopiano, Greg Kearney, Chris DuClos. 2009. Models for assessing uncertainty in local regression. GeoMED. November 14-16. Charleston, South Carolina. (Invited) The underlying statistical methods address a common problem, not only in assessing the association between health and the environment, but also when combining data collected on disparate units, such as some data from counties and others from zip codes. Consequently, frequently this expertise is used to meet other consulting needs. PARTICIPANTS: Linda J. Young is the PI and led this research effort. Florida's Environmental Public Health Tracking Program, which is within the Florida Department of Health, and US Centers for Disease Control and Provention were partner organizations. A graduate student also worked on this project. TARGET AUDIENCES: The primary target audience for the current work is Florida's Environmental Public Health Program, which has a website for all citizens of the state of Florida. Secondarily, the target audiences are the national Environmental Public Health Tracking Program and all states that cooperate with this program. PROJECT MODIFICATIONS: Not relevant to this project.  **Impacts** During 2009-2010, our group has begun to develop a method for properly accounting for the uncertainty when predictions from moving from one geographic scale to another are subsequently used in regression. As an example, for confidentiality reasons, health data are usually reported at the zip code or county level, but air quality is measured at points. To relate a health outcome, such as asthma, to an air quality measurement, say PM2.5, the air quality must first be predicted for the county level. Then a regression of health outcome on air quality, adjusting for demographic and socio-economic factors, is performed. If smoothing methods, such as kriging, are used to predict air quality at the areal-unit level using point-level data, then the estimates of the association between health outcome and air quality are unbiased, but the standard error is severely underestimated. Under this scenario, our group has developed a new approach for analysis that is easy to implement and provides appropriate standard errors under the assumption that the response is normally distributed. Initial efforts have been made to incorporate this approach into Florida's Environmental Public Health Tracking Program. This project provided much needed funding for personnel to conduct this research.  **Publications**   * Lopiano KK, Gotway CA and Young LJ. 2010. A comparison of errors in variables methods for use in regression models with spatially misaligned data. Statistical Methods in Medical Research. Online First as Doi:10.1177/0962280210370266. * Young LJ, Gotway CA and Lopiano KK. 2010. Challenges associated with integrating data from multiple scales to assess relationships. Proceedings of the 9th International Symposium on Spatial Accuracy Assessment in Natural Resources and Environmental Sciences, July 20-23, Leicester, UK. * Young LJ, Gotway CA, Lopiano KK. Kearney G and DuClos C. 2010. Assessing the association between environmental exposures and human health. 19th International Conference on Computational Statistics. August 22-27. Paris, France. |
| **Progress** 10/01/08 to 09/30/09  **Outputs** OUTPUTS: During this period, the research focused on gaining a better understanding of the effect of prediction of environmental exposure on estimating the association between public health and environmental exposure. This problem occurs frequently because environmental exposure is generally measured at points and health is observed either at other points, resulting in misalignment of points, or for areal units. Both are examples of change-of-support. Simulation studies were conducted to provide insight into both problems. Results were presented at three conferences as follows: (1) Gotway, Carol A. and Linda J. Young. 2008. Environmental Public Health Tracking: A Case Study from Florida. Invited Presentation at the Joint Conference of the International Society for Environmental Epidemiology & the International Society of Exposure Analysis. October 12-16. Pasadena, California. (2) Young, Linda J., Carol A. Gotway, Kenneth K. Lopiano, Greg Kearney, Chris DuClos. 2009. Models for assessing uncertainty for local regression: A case study from Florida. Invited presentation at the 6th St. Petersburg Workshop on Simulation. June 27-July 3. St. Petersburg, Russia. (3) Young, Linda J., Carol A. Gotway, Greg Kearney, and Chris DuClos. 2009. Relating public health to environmental factors using data collected from different spatial scales: a case study from Florida. Paper selected for presentation at Tracks2009: the future of environmental health. CDC Tracking Conference. February 24-26. Washington, D.C. PARTICIPANTS: P.I.: Linda J. Young, Department of Statistics, Institute of Food and Agricultural, University of Florida Partner Organizations: Florida Department of Health, U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention TARGET AUDIENCES: The primary target audience are those involved with the U.S. Environmental Public Health Tracking Program. These people tend to be either at the CDC or in state health departments. In addition, others who attempts to relate public health to environmental exposures, including statisticians, epidemiologists, and doctors, are part of the target audience. PROJECT MODIFICATIONS: Nothing significant to report during this reporting period.  **Impacts** Currently, the effect of predicting environmental exposure is generally ignored when assessing the association between public health and environmental exposure. This work shows that, if environmental exposure is predicted using kriging or some other unbiased smoothing approach, the association between public health and environmental exposure can be estimated unbiasedly. However, the standard errors are biased downwards, resulting in some associations being declared significant when they are not. Because the results are to be made publicly available, this type of error can cause undue public concern. The methods that have been proposed to correct the standard error do not always work well.  **Publications**   * Young, Linda J., Carol A. Gotway, Greg Kearney, and Chris DuClos. 2009. Assessing uncertainty in support-adjusted spatial misalignment problems. Communications in Statistics-Theory and Methods 38: 1-16. * Young, Linda J., Carol A. Gotway, Jie Yang, Greg Kearney, and Chris DuClos. 2009. Linking Health and Environmental Data in Geographical Analysis: It is So Much More than Centroids. Spatial and Spatio-temporal Epidemiology 1: 73-84. * Young, Linda J., Carol A. Gotway, Greg Kearney, and Chris DuClos. 2009. Models for Assessing Uncertainty for Local Regression. Proceedings of the 6th St. Petersburg Workshop on Simulation 6: 705-710. |
| **Progress** 05/01/08 to 04/30/09  **Outputs** OUTPUTS: Dissemination has focused on presentations at professional meetings, ranging from statistical to subject-matter scientists. In addition, two presentations were made to personnel in Florida's Department of Health (FDOH). Preparations are being made to put the results on FDOH's website for public dissemination. Contributed Presentations at Professional Meetings: Young, Linda J. and Robert H. Zimmerman. 2008. Modeling mosquito abundance in Brazil using generalized linear mixed models. Joint Statistical Meetings. August 3-7. Denver, Colorado. Young, Linda J., Carol A. Gotway, Greg Kearney, and Chris DuClos. 2009. Relating public health to environmental factors using data collected from different spatial scales: a case study from Florida. Tracks2009: the future of environmental health. CDC Tracking Conference. February 24-26. Washington, D.C. Invited Presentations at Professional Meetings: Gotway, Carol A. and Linda J. Young. 2008. Linking Environmental and Health Data from Different Spatial Scales: A Case Study from Florida's Environmental Public Health Tracking Initiative. 2008 Spring Research Conference on Statistics in Industry and Technology. May 19. Atlanta, Georgia. Gotway, Carol A. and Linda J. Young. 2008. Geostatistics: What's Hot, What's Not and Other Food for Thought. 8th International Symposium on Spatial Accuracy Assessment in Natural Resources and Environmental Sciences. June 25-27. Shanghai, China. Gotway, Carol A. and Linda J. Young. 2008. Linking Environmental Data and Health Data for Environmental Public Health Tracking. Invited for the XXIV International Biometric Conference. July 13-18. Dublin, Ireland. Young, Linda J. and Carol A. Gotway. 2008. Using geostatistical methods in the analysis of public health data: The final frontier geoENV 2008. September 8-10. Southamption, United Kingdom Gotway, Carol A. and Linda J. Young. 2008. Environmental Public Health Tracking: A Case Study from Florida. Joint Conference of the International Society for Environmental Epidemiology & the International Society of Exposure Analysis. October 12-16. Pasadena, California. PARTICIPANTS: This project has been a team activity. Linda J. Young, a professor in the University of Florida's Department of Statistics, is the lead PI on this research. Carol A. Gotway is employed at the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.She collaborates on the theoretical aspects of the work. Greg Kearney and Chris DuClos are employees of Florida's Department of Health and provide epidemiological and mapping support for this project. Jie Yang was a statistics post-doc for this project. TARGET AUDIENCES: Several target audiences exist. This is an active area of research within the statistical community, and results are targeted toward advancing knowledge within that field. The results of the research have broad impacts for health and environmental scientists, personnel within Environmental Public Health Tracking (including those in Florida's Department of Health), and the general public. All of these groups constitute target audiences. PROJECT MODIFICATIONS: Nothing significant to report during this reporting period.  **Impacts** Existing data from multiple sources (e.g., surveillance systems, health registries, governmental agencies) are the foundation for analysis and inference in many studies and programs. More often than not, these data have been collected on different geographical or spatial units, and each of these may be different from the ones of interest. Numerous statistical issues are associated with combining such disparate data. Florida's efforts to move toward implementation of The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)'s Environmental Public Health Tracking (EPHT) Program (EPHT) have highlighted these issues, which are typical of almost any study designed to measure the association between environmental hazards and health outcomes. Accounting for spatial scale when linking data for subsequent inference has been demonstrated to be important in this setting. That is, conclusions may change if the support of the data is not properly accounted for in the analysis. Inferential challenges arise when a potential explanatory variable is measured on one set of spatial units, but then must be predicted on a different set of spatial units. Because uncertainty is associated with prediction of the explanatory variable(s)on the spatial units of interest, measurement error leads to bias in the predicted relationship. Preliminary work has focused on using either a general covariance structure, calibration, and/or geostatistical simulation to reduce or eliminate this bias. The best approach is not currently known, and simulation study is to be conducted determine when each approach may be most appropriate. Random effects and Bayesian models are also under consideration. In all of our work, the focus is on relatively simple methods and concepts that can be transferred to the states' departments of health, the organizations responsible for implementing EPHT.  **Publications**   * Young, Linda J., Carol A. Gotway, Jie Yang, Greg Kearney, and Chris DuClos. 2008. Assessing the association between environmental impacts and health outcomes: a case study from Florida. Statistics in Medicine 27:3998-4015 * Gotway, Carol A. and Linda J. Young. 2008. Geostatistics: What's hot, what's not and other food for thought. (Refereed) Proceedings of the 8th International Symposium on Spatial Accuracy Assessment in Natural Resources and Environmental Sciences. June 25-27. Shanghai, China. |
|

SUMMARY:
Here we summarized the practical statistical methods used in environmental epidemiology to estimate health effects of exposure to multi-pollutant mixture.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Utilize regression analysis to model relationships between health outcomes and environmental factors, identifying significant risk factors using established statistical techniques.
Examine temporal changes in environmental health data, identifying patterns and shifts over time that may indicate emerging health risks related to environmental exposures.
Assess relationships between multiple environmental variables and health outcomes, quantifying strength and direction using correlation coefficients to inform public health interventions.

Leverage statistical software like R and Python for data analysis, facilitating complex computations, simulations, and the application of advanced statistical techniques in environmental health research.
Employ visualization tools to create interactive maps and graphs, enhancing the understanding of data patterns and facilitating communication of findings to stakeholders and the public.
